feedback.text: Student of the Jean Monnet Modules project “DERECHO”: ‘Defense of European Rights and Excellence for Consumers HHarmonisation’, No 101239437. Project financed by the European Union. Jean Monnet Actions in the field of Higher Education: Modules. Call for tenders ERASMUS-JMO-2025-HEI-TCH-RSCH. From my perspective, the Digital Equivalence Act seems to me to be a legal tool that seeks to balance a deeply unequal reality: access to and use of technology. It is not only about everyone being able to have the internet or devices, but that they can fully exercise their rights in digital environments, which today is almost indispensable to study, work or even engage with the public administration. What is most striking to me is that this law seeks not only to close a technological gap, but also a power gap. In a world where information and data are the new strategic resource, those outside the digital environment are also outside social and economic progress. That is why I see digital fairness as a modern way to ensure equality before the law, adapted to the technological age. However, I have some doubt as to how compliance will be monitored and whether there will actually be sufficient resources to implement it throughout the territory, especially in rural areas. A well-drafted law would be of little use if it does not translate into effective and sustained public policies. In short, it is a promising standard, but its true value will be measured in the capacity of the State to make it a tangible reality.
